decisions.title: User API facade
decisions.current_state: fj-api is the V1 user-facing transform facade for jit, grad, vmap, value_and_grad, jacobian, hessian, and make_jaxpr.
decisions.user_outcome: Users get one explicit Rust transform facade now; no extra compatibility wrapper or package rename is introduced in this bead.
decisions.dependency_graph_impact: fj-api depends only on trace/core/dispatch/ad plus dev-only test helpers, so it remains above semantic crates without reverse edges.
decisions.compile_time_impact: Keeping the facade avoids adding another workspace crate and keeps incremental compile churn lower while public examples are still being proven.
decisions.public_api_impact: The public import path remains fj_api::* for V1 evidence; frankenjax package naming is deferred until release packaging work.
decisions.testability_impact: Facade tests stay in fj-api and public example replay is owned by frankenjax-cstq.9.

rejected_options.reason: A wrapper crate would add a second user import surface before README examples are fully replay-proven.
rejected_options.user_impact: Avoids confusing users with two equivalent facade crates and no extra functionality.

guardrails: fj-api must not be a normal dependency of lower-layer semantic crates
public README examples must replay through fj-api under frankenjax-cstq.9

decisions.title: Transform stack ownership
decisions.current_state: Transform proof types live in fj-core, tracing in fj-trace, AD in fj-ad, orchestration in fj-dispatch, and public wrappers in fj-api.
decisions.user_outcome: Users keep current transform behavior while parity work closes advanced control-flow composition gaps.
decisions.dependency_graph_impact: A dedicated fj-transforms crate would currently pull together core, trace, AD, dispatch, and API responsibilities and risks new cycles.
decisions.compile_time_impact: Extraction would force broad rebuilds across the hottest crates without a measured performance or ergonomics win.
decisions.public_api_impact: No transform function moves until frankenjax-cstq.2 and frankenjax-cstq.9 prove the behavior and examples.
decisions.testability_impact: The safer near-term test seam is a conformance matrix over existing crates rather than a structural split.

rejected_options.reason: The advanced transform/control-flow matrix is still open, so extraction would mix semantics work with crate churn.
rejected_options.user_impact: Deferral protects existing jit/grad/vmap behavior and avoids moving APIs while users still need parity evidence.

guardrails: transform-order tests must remain in dispatch/conformance gates
fj-core proof types must not depend on fj-dispatch or fj-api
revisit_when: public README examples replay through fj-api

decisions.title: Lowering and execution boundary
decisions.current_state: V1 lowers to canonical IR evaluated by interpreter/dispatch paths; fj-backend-cpu owns dependency-wave CPU execution and fj-runtime owns admission policy.
decisions.user_outcome: Users keep the documented CPU-only interpreter/backend semantics without an invented XLA-like lowering layer.
decisions.dependency_graph_impact: A new fj-lowering crate would be mostly policy glue today and would increase cross-crate coupling with dispatch/backend.
decisions.compile_time_impact: Deferral avoids moving interpreter/backend code while performance and memory gates are still being expanded.
decisions.public_api_impact: No user-facing lowering API is exposed in V1; claims remain CPU/interpreter/backend scoped.
decisions.testability_impact: Execution behavior remains covered by interpreter, dispatch, backend, and conformance tests with explicit E2E logs.

rejected_options.reason: There is no separate XLA/compiler lowering contract in V1; extracting a crate would formalize a surface users cannot use yet.
rejected_options.user_impact: Avoids overpromising a compiler pipeline and keeps the README limitation honest.

guardrails: README must continue to state no XLA lowering for V1
backend execution must stay covered by conformance and performance gates
revisit_when: a real compiler/lowering IR is introduced
backend parity requires multiple execution backends

decisions.title: CPU backend boundary
decisions.current_state: fj-backend-cpu is a dedicated CPU backend crate and fj-runtime owns backend registry/admission concerns.
decisions.user_outcome: Users get a concrete always-available CPU backend without waiting for GPU/TPU abstractions.
decisions.dependency_graph_impact: The backend stays above core/interpreter/lax/runtime and below dispatch/api, preserving layered flow.
decisions.compile_time_impact: Keeping the backend crate isolated localizes Rayon-heavy backend rebuilds.
decisions.public_api_impact: Backend internals remain implementation detail unless explicitly promoted later.
decisions.testability_impact: Backend scheduling can be tested and benchmarked independently from facade examples.

rejected_options.reason: Dispatch already coordinates transforms/cache/ledger; folding backend scheduling into it would enlarge the integration choke point.
rejected_options.user_impact: Keeping it separate preserves focused performance gates and simpler failure diagnosis.

guardrails: fj-backend-cpu must not depend on fj-api or fj-conformance
backend performance changes require benchmark evidence

decisions.title: FFI boundary
decisions.current_state: fj-ffi isolates C ABI registration, buffers, calls, callbacks, and error propagation.
decisions.user_outcome: Users get one auditable native interop boundary and the rest of the workspace stays unsafe-free.
decisions.dependency_graph_impact: fj-ffi depends only on fj-core in normal builds, so native interop cannot pull execution or harness crates downward.
decisions.compile_time_impact: The FFI crate compiles independently from dispatch/conformance-heavy paths.
decisions.public_api_impact: FFI symbols remain opt-in and do not affect the Rust transform facade.
decisions.testability_impact: FFI adversarial tests remain localized and can be fuzzed without changing pure Rust crates.

rejected_options.reason: Putting native calls into runtime would weaken the single unsafe-boundary doctrine.
rejected_options.user_impact: Users retain a simpler security story and clearer audit surface.

guardrails: unsafe code remains isolated to fj-ffi test/FFI implementation surfaces
production crates must not depend on fj-ffi unless an explicit interop feature is added

decisions.title: Conformance and evidence harness
decisions.current_state: fj-conformance owns oracle fixtures, parity reports, durability tools, E2E forensic logs, and architecture gates.
decisions.user_outcome: Users and agents get one evidence plane for replaying claims without production crates depending on the harness.
decisions.dependency_graph_impact: Conformance stays at the top layer and may depend on implementation crates, never the reverse.
decisions.compile_time_impact: Evidence binaries and tests can build separately from core library consumers.
decisions.public_api_impact: Harness APIs are evidence infrastructure, not user-facing transform APIs.
decisions.testability_impact: All child evidence lanes can share forensic log validation and artifact hashing.

rejected_options.reason: Distributed gate binaries would fragment log schema enforcement and replay commands.
rejected_options.user_impact: Centralizing the evidence plane gives users one place to inspect red/yellow/green status.

guardrails: no non-conformance crate may depend normally on fj-conformance
new evidence scripts must emit shared E2E forensic logs or an explicit adapter
